updates.summary: Amazon's $10 billion data center campus in Canton, the largest capital investment in Mississippi history, occupies 1,713 acres across two sites in Madison County, with 927 acres at the Madison County Mega Site off Highway 22 and Nissan Parkway. The campus is expected to create 1,000 high-wage jobs. In partnership with Entergy, AWS will bring 650 megawatts of new renewable energy. Idlebriar subdivision residents, less than 200 yards from the campus, report dust, traffic, noise, and inability to sell homes. Amazon offered free fiber internet and car washes as mitigation.

During a rare winter storm on January 22, 2025, Marion County Council approved a $2.4B data center under the code name 'Project Liberty' while most residents were snowed in and unaware. Stream Data Centers, operating through Eagle Myra LLC, plans a two-phase project with up to six buildings on the county's industrial park on U.S. Highway 501 Bypass. Stream estimates only 20 permanent jobs and 7,500 gallons/day water usage for the first phase. Council members had signed NDAs barring public disclosure. Marion County is predominantly Black, and the case became a national symbol of how developers use NDAs and secrecy to push projects through communities with less organized political power.

DC BLOX acquired 55 acres and broke ground on a 180MW data center campus in Douglasville, featuring twelve data halls totaling 750,000 sq ft. The campus is strategically located next to Microsoft's 300-acre facility. In August 2025, DC BLOX closed $1.15 billion in green loan financing for construction. Douglas County subsequently approved a moratorium on data center development.

In March 2025, the Loudoun County Board of Supervisors voted 7-2 to approve a comprehensive plan amendment and zoning ordinance amendment eliminating by-right data center development. All new data center applications now require staff-level review, a public hearing, and Planning Commission recommendation. Previously, data centers could be approved with only staff-level review in some zones. Loudoun Climate Project and the Virginia Data Center Reform Coalition (which includes the Piedmont Environmental Council, Sierra Club, and National Parks Conservation Association) pushed for these restrictions.

On June 10, 2025, the Henrico County Board of Supervisors adopted stricter data center requirements, creating a 3,100-acre White Oak Technology Park overlay as the only zone where data centers can be built without a Provisional Use Permit (PUP). Outside this zone, all data center proposals require PUPs with public hearings. New requirements include restrictions on backup generator testing hours, mandatory enclosure of noise-generating equipment, and closed-loop water-cooling systems. The Board also denied a zoning appeal from Centra Logistics for a data center outside the overlay.

Project Sail is a 900MW, $17B proposed hyperscale data center on 832 acres in northwest Coweta County by Prologis/Atlas Development. Citizens for Rural Coweta formed in early January 2025 at Sargent Baptist Church and grew to 3,800+ members. The Stop Project Sail campaign gathered 2,245 petition signatures. Rae Lynn Butler, secretary of culture and humanities for the Muscogee Nation, opposed the project due to historic Muscogee Nation land. The project would consume 9 million gallons of water per day. Coweta County passed a moratorium on data center development.

The PW Digital Gateway proposes rezoning 2,100+ acres near Manassas Battlefield for up to 18 million sq ft of data centers (10.5M by Compass, 7.9M by QTS), supporting 1,471 to 5,048 jobs. A judge voided the $24.7B rezoning and the Virginia Court of Appeals barred construction pending appeal. The Coalition to Protect Prince William County and American Battlefield Trust lead opposition. The project strongly encourages closed-loop cooling systems to minimize water impact.

Amazon Web Services is building a $500M data center on 243 acres along Route 3 in Stevensburg, Culpeper County, with two buildings totaling 427,000 sq ft and approximately 90MW capacity. The project anticipates 90 permanent jobs (45 per building) and 365 construction workers. Board of Supervisors approved the rezoning 4-3 despite Planning Commission voting 5-4 against. Coalition to Save Culpeper, Save Brandy Station, and Piedmont Environmental Council filed a lawsuit alleging illegal spot-zoning.

DeKalb County approved a 100-day moratorium on data center applications in July 2024, later extended multiple times through June 2026. Commissioner Edward 'Ted' Terry warned residential power bills could rise $20/month. Renew DeKalb, led by organizer Gina Mangham, rallied residents to urge a ban on hyperscale data center campuses. About 100 residents attended meetings opposing data centers near Doraville, Lithonia, and school campuses. DeKalb created a first-of-its-kind ordinance with different categories based on square footage and power demand.

Compass Data Centers proposed a $1B+ campus on 333 acres of farmland on Stamey Farm Road in western Iredell County. Plans include five buildings of approximately 270,000 sq ft each (1.35M total) with 40 backup generators per building. A Facebook group with 900+ members organized opposition citing air and groundwater pollution and property value impacts. Despite opposition, the Statesville City Council unanimously approved the project in September 2025.

Project Tango proposes the first hyperscale data center in Palm Beach County on 202 acres at the Central Park Commerce Center in Loxahatchee. PBA Holdings (subsidiary of Palm Beach Aggregates LLC, managed by Enrique Tomeu) and TPA Group (Atlanta-based, led by J. Bradford Smith) are the developers. The project could total 3.69 million sq ft at full build-out, including a 1.8 million sq ft AI data center. The end user (likely Amazon, Microsoft, or Google) is hidden behind the code name. Commissioners voted 7-0 to postpone the application until April 2026 for more impact studies.

Thomas A. Hobbs Living Trust requested rezoning of a 138-acre site along Joe Prather Highway (25025 Joe Prather Hwy, Ekron) from Agricultural/Residential to Light Industrial for data center development. After a 5-hour public hearing with 150+ residents and a 2,800-signature petition, the Planning Commission declined to recommend rezoning. Fiscal Court unanimously blocked the proposal and subsequently adopted a moratorium (Ordinance 2025-07) on new data center and IT infrastructure facility applications.

updates.company: Poe Companies / PowerHouse Data Centers (approved project)
updates.summary: One 525MW data center project in southwest Louisville has been cleared to proceed, built by Poe Companies and PowerHouse Data Centers (starting at 100MW, expanding to 400MW). District 17 Council Member Markus Winkler proposed a six-month moratorium on September 11, 2025, which was tabled indefinitely. A survey showed 81.5% of respondents chose 'Nowhere in Jefferson County' for data center locations. City planners continue drafting regulations.

Elon Musk's xAI built 'Colossus,' the world's largest AI supercomputer, in Boxtown, a 90% Black neighborhood settled by formerly enslaved people in 1863. The facility operates 33 methane-powered gas turbines (permitted for only 15) without appropriate Clean Air Act permits, increasing Memphis's smog-forming NOx emissions by 30-60%. University of Tennessee researchers found peak nitrogen dioxide levels increased 79% near the facility. Over 2,000 public comments were submitted opposing the turbines. SELC filed a 60-day notice of intent to sue for Clean Air Act violations on behalf of NAACP. xAI leased 552 acres and plans to expand to 300MW+ consuming 5.7 million gallons of water per day. MLGW electricity bills averaged $4.5 million/month. Approximately 320 employees estimated.

xAI is building 'MACROHARDRR,' a $20 billion data center in Southaven, the largest economic development project in Mississippi history. The facility will be over 800,000 sq ft on Stateline Road, expected to begin operations in February 2026. The investment will provide 'easily' over 1,000 jobs to DeSoto County. This is xAI's third facility in the Mid-South region, located near its newly acquired power plant site and its existing Memphis data center.

Project Marvel is a $14.5B, 4.5 million sq ft data center on 700 acres requiring 1,200 megawatts of power (enough to power 760,000 homes). Construction over 7-8 years will create 1,000 construction jobs; 330 permanent jobs with $112,550 average salary. Logistics Land Investment LLC and TPA Group (Atlanta) are the developers. The NAACP (Alabama State Conference, Metro Birmingham Branch, Center for Environmental and Climate Justice) formally opposed the project. Bessemer, approximately 70% Black with a legacy of pollution, changed its laws in October 2025 to accommodate data centers despite 7,000+ petition signatures against.

Cielo Digital Infrastructure is investing $2.1 billion in an 86-acre campus off Ford Road in Gaffney, engineered for up to 300MW of IT load. Four facilities of approximately 400,000 sq ft each (1.6M total) with a dedicated on-site electrical substation. The project will create 30 new jobs. Initial operations expected online by year-end 2028. A regional petition opposing Upstate SC data centers has gathered approximately 4,000 signatures.

QTS is investing $1 billion in York County to build its first South Carolina data center campus. QTS acquired approximately 400 acres in 2023 and purchased another 400 acres in December 2025 for $26M, totaling 800 acres. Nine buildings are planned with four under construction in phase one. The campus features carbon-free power and water-free cooling technology that uses zero water. Residents have voiced concerns at community meetings.

Warrenton Town Council voted to fully ban data centers after significant community opposition. GI Partners had purchased a $39M, 42-acre plot for a 220,000 sq ft data center. Over 500 people attended a Town Council meeting with nearly 130 speakers opposing the project, including Oscar winner Robert Duvall. Citizens for Fauquier County (CFFC) filed multiple lawsuits, including challenges over the special use permit and withheld emails.

Western Hospitality Partners proposed 'Project Lincoln: OC Data Center,' a $6B, 600MW facility initially planned for 267 acres at 3557 North Highway 53. Due to fierce opposition from 'We Are Oldham County,' the company tried switching to a smaller 30-acre drive-in movie theater site. Oldham County passed a 150-day moratorium 4-2, and Western Hospitality Partners subsequently withdrew its application entirely.

Natelli Investments LLC filed plans for the 'New Hill Digital Campus,' a 250MW, $1B+ data center complex on 190 acres off Old U.S. Highway 1 near Apex, featuring four 200,000 sq ft buildings (800,000 sq ft total). The facility would consume up to 1 million gallons of reclaimed water per day during peak summer heat. Over 4,500 people signed a petition opposing the project. Residents formed the Protect Wake County Coalition. The site is adjacent to the Shearon Harris Nuclear Plant and will support approximately 3,000 construction jobs.

Franks Investment Company LLC / SAC III Acquisition Co proposed a 2.8 million sq ft data center at the Resilient Technology Park (7340 Greenwood Road) in West Shreveport. Documents show the facility would use 7.5 million gallons of potable water per day. The Shreveport Metropolitan Planning Commission denied the special use permit 4-4 in December 2025. The City Council overturned the denial. Residents filed a lawsuit accusing the city of violating constitutional obligations, noting the operator's identity remains unknown to the public.

Serverfarm acquired 135 acres through subsidiary SF ARK1 LLC, north of the city's water treatment plant on Palmer Road. Plans call for six buildings across 135 acres totaling over 2 million sq ft. Redacted load letters reference 318MW, 307MW, and a 450MW bridge phase. The project could cost $8 billion at full build-out. Residents are calling for a pause, public hearing, and stating 'Arkansas land, water, and communities are not for sale.' A Change.org petition has been launched to stop the project.

Eagle Rock Partners proposed an 859-acre, 1,000-megawatt, $6 billion data center on Cooks Hill Road near the ACE Basin. The project would cover timberland and 200 acres of wetlands. Plans include 500 permanent jobs (30% IT, 30% facilities, 30% security, 10% admin). Coastal Conservation League and SELC have opposed the project. Landowners filed a lawsuit against Colleton County over the data center ordinance. State legislators from Colleton County also oppose the project.

opposition_groups: No Data Centers in Stokes County
updates.summary: Project Delta by Engineered Land Solutions proposes an 1,800-acre AI data center campus in Walnut Cove near the Dan River, with 998 acres to be developed and the rest as floodplain. The campus would total 'several hundred megawatts' with a new dedicated substation. The county would collect an estimated $40 million in annual property taxes (current tax bill is under $8,000). Stokes County Board of Commissioners approved the rezoning 3-2 in January 2026 despite opposition from Clean Water for North Carolina and a local No Data Centers petition.

Mesh Capital LLC sought to remove a protective proffer from a 57-acre property on Cabin Field Road. Supervisors voted 5-2 to deny the request in October 2025. However, since data centers have by-right use on heavy industrial zoned properties, the developer submitted three new site plans for a scaled-back 450,000 sq ft data center that staff determined to be in compliance with the proffer.

TenKey LandCo I LLC proposed a nearly $5 billion data center on approximately 200 acres near Exit 2 of I-65 at the southern end of Franklin. Plans include three 200,000 sq ft buildings ($1.6B per facility) to be developed over three years, with at least 100 jobs at $80,000 minimum salary and $17.68 million in projected tax revenue. TenKey filed a lawsuit against Simpson County in January 2026 challenging a county ordinance regulating advanced technology centers. Zoning regulators have tabled the vote multiple times.

TigerDC planned 'Project Spero,' a $3 billion, 400MW data center at Tyger River Industrial Park along Highway 290. Hundreds of residents opposed at public hearings and thousands signed a petition. Federal regulators denied a proposal for Lockhart Power to purchase 200MW from Duke Energy, citing potential cost pass-through to customers. After Spartanburg County Council voted against offering tax incentives, TigerDC formally withdrew Project Spero on February 27, 2026, and began evaluating alternative sites.

DC BLOX is the company behind the Lowndes County data center proposal. DC BLOX's CEO informed the landowner (Pope Langdale) the facility could create up to 400 jobs with $135,000 average salary and would use closed-loop cooling. Lowndes Citizens Against Data Centers (LCAD), organized with Alpha Kappa Alpha Upsilon and the Valdosta State University Anthropology Club, hosted a town hall where citizens pushed back on the potential development. In fall 2025, a parcel was rezoned for the potential data center.

A Fortune 20 tech company (identity hidden behind NDAs) proposed a $1 billion, 1,200-megawatt data center on up to 5,000 acres of farmland on Big Pond Pike near Maysville. The project could bring 400 full-time positions plus 1,500 construction jobs with $80,000 average salary. The massive power requirement nearly equals the output of Eastern Kentucky Power Cooperative's Spurlock power plant. Town leaders signed NDAs barring public disclosure, sparking controversy. Rules governing the project moved toward a final vote in February 2026.

Atlas Development filed a Development of Regional Impact for 'Project Bus,' a 12-building data center campus on approximately 350 acres in Temple (northern Carroll County). The estimated $11 billion project would generate $42 million/year in ad valorem taxes and 275 jobs. The project requires rezoning, annexation, and sewer connections. Properties span Vaughn Road, Center Point Road, Terrell Road, and Mandeville Road, north and south of I-20, about 40 miles west of downtown Atlanta.

QTS Data Centers plans a $2 billion data center at 2883 Gordon Highway that could be twice the size of Augusta Mall. The company filed to expand to six two-story buildings totaling over 2.15 million sq ft. The project is expected to create 160-220 jobs and could be completed by Q2 2026. The project flew 'under the radar' until local news coverage revealed its scale, surprising neighbors.

Denver-based Tract proposed the $30B Mooresville Technology Park on 400 acres of land owned by Teresa Earnhardt, widow of NASCAR champion Dale Earnhardt. Planned capacity was 1.5GW (1,500MW) with 500,000 gallons/day water usage. Dale Earnhardt's eldest son Kerry posted a YouTube video opposing the project. 'No to Mooresville Technology Park' organized opposition through a website and Facebook group. About 200 people crowded a Board meeting wearing red T-shirts. Tract withdrew the rezoning request on August 13, 2025.

NorthPoint Development (Kansas City) proposed a 664-acre, 14-building hyperscale data center with 1,008MW peak power demand and 3 million sq ft in the small riverside community of fewer than 2,000 people. The projected electricity consumption of 8.8 million megawatt-hours per year is more than half the output of the Gaston Plant (1,880MW). Over 100 residents packed a council meeting to oppose. A Change.org petition gathered 500+ signatures. No formal application has been filed but residents are organizing preemptively.

Little Rock approved annexation and rezoning under the code name 'Project Boar.' AVAIO Digital Partners (Stamford, CT) later announced a 760-acre campus with $6B first phase investment and over $21B through full development. The campus will create 500+ permanent operations jobs over five years. Entergy has contracted 150MW to be energized in Q2 2027. Arkansas Grass Roots United and planning commissioners objected to the secrecy, lack of public input, and potential environmental impacts.

Franklin County Commission denied rezoning for Red Dog Technologies' proposed Bitcoin mining data center near Tullahoma. Residents said noise would be unbearable and damage property values. The denial was one of the early examples of local opposition to crypto mining data centers in Tennessee.
